For example, the authors should have mentioned how $threshold$ is calculated when it appears for the first time – In the paper, it is not defined until section 6, which can confuse the readers.

The method implicitly encodes sparse attention patterns, since the attention bias $\tilde A_{i,j}$ can be set to $-\infty$ for a large number of tokens. While it can be helpful for simple tasks, I’m not convinced of its effectiveness in more general settings (e.g., for natural language). I'm worried ABC can only be effective for a small number of tasks. The tasks in this paper also seemed to be oversimplified. I'm wondering if ABC can still work well for multi-digit multiplication rather than the simple $N\times 1$.

ABC is only compared against weak baselines in the experiments. As discussed in the paper, existing papers have shown Alibi and RoPE are suboptimal on algorithmic tasks. The authors should compare ABC against stronger baselines, e.g., Randomized Positional Encodings, RoPE with Position Interpolation, etc.
Typos.
Footnote 4, “16 etc” $\to$ “16, etc”.
Footnote 6, “tasks” $\to$ “tasks.”.
Can ABC still work well for more complex task, e.g., multi-digit multiplication, or even natural language modeling?
Can you compare ABC with more advanced baselines?

The important results from section 4 are hard to understand if one only reads the main paper. In particular, the claim that constrained attention solves parity is only supported in the appendix. Also the logic behind section 5 is hard to comprehend without reading parts of section A.4 (the precise description of windowed attention, and the cyclic encoding). The paper would be made much stronger by moving a significant part of appendices A.4 and A.5 into section 4. This can probably be done at the expense of figure 1 (not very informative), and parts of section 5.

The claim that constrained attention, and ABC, offers a **complete** solution to length generalization on problems of arithmetic is exaggerated. All the tasks considered in the paper share a common trait: their solution can be computed by looking at just a few consecutive digits of the problem and the currently computed solution. This would break for such a basic arithmetic task as summing $n$ k-digit integers, and generalizing on larger $n$. As for multiplication, $N	imes 1$ multiplication is closer to $10$ unary operation than a binary operation. $N	imes N$ multiplication would have the same non local behavior.

Given the comments in section 7, about the similarity between ABC and RPE, it is regrettable that the experiments in section 4 do not feature RPE (except addition, which corresponds to a different architecture). It would be interesting, in particular, to see how RPE perform on the successor and parity task.
You use 1/6 layer transformers, what is the benefit of a shallow encoder, compared to a (smaller) 6/1 encoder, or 4/4 model?
All your vanilla models use the cosine positional embeddings. You observe that it sometimes interferes with the sliding window attention, could you try learnable PE?
Could you try RPE on the successor and parity experiments? Your conclusions suggest that it would help generalize.
In the experiments from appendix A.5 (table 3), you try cyclic PE on top of a w=1 windowed attention, what happens if you use it without windowed attention? (it is another form of RPE)
Have you tried $N	imes N$ multiplication (for smaller values of $N$)? of $N	imes k$ for $k$ larger than 3 (so that the model cannot memorize all cases as so many unary operations)?
The Jelassi results in table 3 and figure 1 correspond to a different architecture (encoder-only, with shared layers), it would be better to rerun them, or at least to clarify this.

The inductive bias is very strong: it biases the attention matrix to be diagonal lines. The method might not work in more general cases in 2 different ways: it might not help with generalization, but it might also hinder learning (I strongly suspect that this would be the case with language-related tasks). Thus, the limitations of the method are not clear. I think this is the biggest weakness of the paper.

The authors do not cite some relevant work that has been shown to help with extrapolating to longer sequences [1, 2], for example by Transformer-XL style attention [3]. Other work already showed extrapolation on some algorithmic tasks, for example, ListOps and modular arithmetic [4]. Please cite it.

Adding a task where the attention pattern should not be a straight line would be interesting, to see the limitations of the method. One interesting task could be ListOps (for an analysis of attention patterns, see [4] from the "Weaknesses" chapter)

On page 6, when explaining how the attention matrices are extracted, it is written "are parameter matrices in the last decoder layer". Does this mean that only the last decoder layer has the attention bias? Or do all of them have it, but set to the same as the last layer?

The absolute PEs are usually not injected in the attention head directly, but to the residual stream immediately after the token embeddings. Given this, in the first eq. on P6, what are the additive p_i and p_j components? Do you do a custom method, where you inject the PE directly into the attention at each layer?
How do you handle the cross-attention? Does that have this bias as well?

Have you tried a less aggressive bias, where you don't set the elements to -inf, but to some finite negative number, such that the network has the chance to overcome the bias if it is good for the task? Do you think it would work in this case?

The authors achieve perfect length generalization on algorithmic tasks (x+1, x+y, and N x 1). The models are trained with as little as a sequence length of 6, and they generalize to sequences with a length of 5. They achieve this by learning an attention pattern on short sequences which interpolates and extrapolating the attention pattern to long sequences, in the form of an additive bias. This bias is somewhat learned, however it has a very strong structural prior in the form of lines in the attention matrix.

I'm willing to raise my score if my questions about the positioning of the biases are cleared, and if the authors could run their method on any task that does not require strictly monotonic attention (e.g ListOps), regardless of the outcome of the experiment. Just to make it clear what are the limitations.

The paper explores the capabilities of transformer models in learning arithmetic algorithms, specifically focusing on generalization to long input lengths. It introduces two methods: Attention Bias Scaffolding (ABS) and Attention Bias Calibration (ABC), aimed at enhancing transformer models' ability to generalize to longer inputs. The paper presents extensive experiments on various arithmetic tasks, demonstrating that the proposed methods can achieve perfect generalization to inputs with up to 50 digits. The authors also discuss the attention patterns learned by transformers and how these patterns can be leveraged to improve generalization. The paper is well-written, clear, and presents a novel approach to addressing the challenge of length generalization in transformers.
The paper is well-written, clear, and easy to follow, with detailed explanations of the methods and experiments.
The experiments are extensive, covering various tasks and showing the effectiveness of the proposed methods.
The paper addresses an important and challenging problem in the field of transformers, specifically focusing on length generalization in arithmetic tasks.
The proposed methods, such as Attention Bias Scaffolding (ABS) and Attention Bias Calibration (ABC), are novel and provide a new perspective on enhancing transformer models' capabilities.
The paper provides a comprehensive analysis of attention patterns and their impact on model performance, which is a significant contribution to the field.
The paper's focus on arithmetic tasks, while interesting, may limit the generalizability of the findings to other types of tasks or domains.
The methods proposed, such as windowed attention biasing and cyclic position indexing, are not entirely novel and have been previously explored in other works.
The paper could benefit from a broader discussion on the limitations and societal impacts of the proposed methods.
The experimental setup and results are somewhat limited to specific tasks and model configurations, which might not fully demonstrate the generalizability of the findings.
The paper could be improved by including more complex tasks and a broader range of models to validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods across different scenarios.
Could you clarify the term "successor" as used in the paper?
How do the proposed methods perform on other tasks beyond arithmetic, such as language modeling?
What are the potential negative societal impacts of the proposed methods, and how might they be mitigated?
How does the paper's methodology compare to other state-of-the-art methods, particularly in terms of computational efficiency and scalability?
Could you elaborate on the methodology used for the experimental results presented in Table 1, particularly the use of bold text to highlight the best accuracies achieved for certain input lengths?
3 good

7 accept, but needs minor improvements

The paper presents a novel approach to addressing the challenge of length generalization in transformers, using methods such as Attention Bias Scaffolding (ABS) and Attention Bias Calibration (ABC). The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of these methods in achieving perfect generalization to inputs with up to 50 digits, which is a significant achievement. The paper is well-written, clear, and provides a comprehensive analysis of attention patterns, which is a valuable contribution to the field. However, the paper could benefit from a broader discussion on the limitations and societal impacts of the proposed methods, as well as more extensive experiments to validate the generalizability of the findings. The decision to accept is based on the paper's originality, methodological soundness, and the significance of its results, despite some concerns about the scope of the experiments and the potential for broader application of the findings.

All the reviewers are concerned about the theoretical guarantees, including the identifiability of this method, and the authors would need to address the raised critical concerns related to the theoretical aspects.

The authors did not offer an analysis of the identifiability of the latent causal model. Without theoretical identifiability results, it becomes challenging to have full confidence in the outcomes generated by their proposed method.
The experimental results only demonstrated a basic setting with five nodes, which may not be sufficient to provide a comprehensive empirical study.
Regarding the number of latent variables: How can we get the number of latent variables? Do we need to know it in advance?
Regarding the intervention: Is the intervening variable only on the observed variable? Can we intervet the latent variables?
Regarding the experiments: What is the performance of different setting graphs?

This paper introduces an empirical estimation method for inferring latent causal relationships within the framework of causal representation learning. It focuses on assuming linear latent causal models and formulates the problem as a Bayesian inference task for these models.

The novelty of this work appears somewhat constrained. It focuses solely on the scenario where the latent causal model is linear, interventions on latent variables are assumed, and the intervention targets are known.
Furthermore, it does not explicitly clarify whether this setting is theoretically identifiable.
The experimental validation is somewhat lacking. The paper only presents results with 5 latent variables, which is not enough for an empirical study.
1. It is crucial for the authors to explicitly establish whether this setting is theoretically identifiable prior to introducing empirical estimation methods.

2. The paper would greatly benefit from a more comprehensive set of experiments. This should include exploring different numbers of latent variables, varying graph densities, and adjusting sample sizes for a more thorough assessment.

The authors should provide more explanations about the model architecture in Figure 2. Some description in the caption would help the reader to understand the whole algorithm while reading the introduction section.
A high-level description of the existing Bayesian structure learning methods mentioned in Section 4.2 can be provided for the reader’s convenience.
The ancestral sampling method should be described more explicitly.

It is not specifically mentioned about the 3-layer neural network in Section 5: Experiments. For example, what type of layers did the authors use? What is their dimension, and what activation functions were used? The training details can be provided in the appendix section.

Although the authors discussed different recent approaches in their related work section, they did not show how their approach is different from those and how their approaches outperform earlier works. For example, the authors cited Brehmer et al. (2022) who identify the causal structure and disentangle causal variables for arbitrary, unknown causal graphs with observations before and after intervention. The assumptions in this work seem to be more relaxed than the assumptions mentioned in this paper (linear Gaussian assumption, known intervention). It is not clearly specified what improvement this paper is doing compared to previous works.

The authors did not discuss the identifiability of the SCM and the causal structure in detail. There is no theoretical guarantee that the algorithm will learn to sample true SCM and the causal structure. For example, the author’s one cited work Brehmer et al. (2022) claims that they can find SCMs identifiable up to a relabelling and elementwise reparameterizations of the causal variables. Without any theoretical identifiability, what is the guarantee that the algorithm will not overfit the training datasets? What is the guarantee that the resultant SCM and structure will match the interventional distribution (ex: distribution shift) that was absent in the training data?

Although the authors discussed some approaches that deal with causal representation learning problems in the related work section, it is unclear why they could not find any common ground to show where they could show their comparative performance.

The authors showed their performance on a 5-node DAG with 20 random interventions. The authors should perform more intensive experiments such as: for small to large graphs with varying edge density and varying interventions.
The authors used only synthetic and comparatively less complex datasets. The algorithm performance would be better observed on more complicated datasets such as Causal3DIdent [1] etc.
How does this algorithm fail when the SCM is not linear Gaussian additive noise SCM?
How is d: the number of latent variables known?
In a real-world setting, if Z are latent variables, how the interventions are known? How is it determined which latent variables are being intervened on?
The question about overfitting and finding the unique/identifiable SCM that will match unseen interventions absent in the training data.
How are the loss terms being calculated in Algorithm 1 lines 14,15?

How are \hat{L} and \hat{\sigma} being sampled at Algorithm 1 line 3? How is gradient descent being done without breaking the computational graph since the author performs sampling at lines 3 and 10? Is the loss terms differentiable with respect to \phi and \theta even though sampling is performed?
What is the role of interventional datasets? How would the algorithm’s performance change when the available data is from more or less number of interventions?
How does the algorithm perform for different-sized DAGs with varying edge density and varying interventions?
What is the causal structure for the Chemistry dataset? It should be precisely mentioned.
the paper has been critiqued for its lack of novelty, limited scalability, and inadequate experimental validation against state-of-the-art methods.
Concerns were also raised about the clarity of the methodological presentation and the need for more robust empirical evidence to support the claims.
The paper lacks a detailed discussion on the limitations of the proposed method, which is crucial for understanding its applicability and potential shortcomings.
The novelty of the proposed method is questionable as it appears to be a combination of existing methods without significant innovation.
The experimental evaluation is limited, and the results are not convincingly superior to existing methods, particularly in terms of scalability and performance on more complex datasets.
There are several minor typographical and grammatical errors that need correction to enhance the professional quality of the paper.

The paper could benefit from a more comprehensive discussion on the identifiability of the causal model, especially given the assumptions made about the structure and parameters of the latent causal model.
How does the proposed method compare with existing methods in terms of scalability and performance on more complex datasets?
Can the authors provide more details on the identifiability of the causal model, especially under the assumptions made about the structure and parameters of the latent causal model?
What are the specific advantages of the proposed method over existing methods, particularly in terms of computational efficiency and accuracy of causal structure recovery?
How does the method perform when the number of latent variables is larger than the number of observable variables?
Could the authors clarify the role and impact of the noise in the model, particularly in scenarios where the noise is not fixed?
How does the method handle the issue of potential overfitting to the training data, especially in scenarios where the data is limited?
The primary concerns include the lack of novelty, limited scalability, and inadequate experimental validation against state-of-the-art methods.
The reviewers also highlighted the need for more robust empirical evidence to support the claims made by the authors.
Furthermore, the presentation of the methodology and the clarity of the experimental results were noted as needing improvement.

This work presents a method for performing feature selection in deep learning models called DeepDRK, building on top of Model-X knockoffs (Barber & Candes, 2015). The authors note that while a variety of methods have been developed to use knockoffs in deep learning models, the methods are not robust to different distributions of data, are hard to train and perform poorly under small numbers of observations. Thus they proposed a regularized version of knockoffs known as DeepDRK. DeepDRK uses a vision transformer as the underlying model architecture and is designed to maintain the 'swap property' from Barber & Candes while minimizing reconstructability. They also develop an adversarial training procedure called 'multi-swapper'.

The reviewers found the paper sound overall, well written and easy to follow. They seemed to agree that feature selection for deep learning was important and interesting. The review scores were quite borderline (6, 6, 5, 6) with very low variance. Unfortunately, no reviewer was willing to champion the paper, and similarly none were strongly opposed to acceptance.
There was a consensus among reviewers that there were insufficient ablations done to justify the various modeling and algorithmic choices proposed in the paper.

Unfortunately, given that no reviewers were willing to champion the paper (some did respond during the response phase that they were unwilling to change their scores), the paper falls below the bar for acceptance. Hopefully the reviews will help strengthen the paper for a future submission.
I don't see any major weakness in this work.
I have no more questions. I am not an expert in this field, but I feel this paper is good from the perspective of general machine learning.

The paper proposed “Deep Dependency Regularized Knockoff (DeepDRK)”, a distribution-free deep learning method that strikes a balance between FDR and power. It leverages transformer architecture and several loss functions for training to generate Knockoff.

Some arguments of the proposed method is not validated with corresponding experimental results. For example, “multi-swapper” is used to better achieve swap property. But there are no experiments to justify the how swap property changes when changing from single swapper to multi-swapper. I think authors should also introduce how to empirically measure the swap property. Since the proposed method relies on regularization to enforce the swap property, which is not guaranteed by design.

The proposed method uses many regularization terms. Some of the regularization terms have ablation studies, but others are not. For example, L_swapper and L_ED are not included. The effect of α in Eq.~(9) is also not investigated. Moreover, there are four hyperparameters require tuning, making the proposed method hard to tune.
The regularization terms largely come from existing papers; I think authors should better justify what is their contribution on top of existing papers.
Since most dataset is not very large, but the model size is quite large. Did authors try to change the model size to see how it impacts the performance? Maybe the model could be smaller.
The necessity of introducing these five losses is under-explored in the paper.
The necessity of DRP is unclear. There lacks comparison of $\tilde{X} _{\theta} and\tilde{X}_{\theta}^{DRP}$ in empirical performance.
It is unclear how well the knockoffs generated by DeepDRK following the swap property and avoid overfitting compared to baseline methods.

The results w.r.t. the Gaussian mixture seems inconsistent with that in the original DDLK paper (DDLK performs the worst in this paper while it performs better than deep knockoffs and knockoffgan in the original paper).
To avoid overfitting, why introducing a post-training perturbation instead of modifying training strategy like early stopping or tuning hypermeters?

The authors develop a distribution-free deep learning method for knockoff generation which strikes a balance between FDR and power, called “Deep Dependency Regularized Knockoff” (DeepDRK). In DeepDRK, a “multi-swapper” adversarial training procedure is proposed to enforce the swap property, while a sliced-Wasserstein-based dependency regularization (together with a novel perturbation technique) is introduced to reduce reconstructability. Experiments on real, synthetic, and semi-synthetic datasets are carried out to show the good performance.

Though it enjoys theoretical result that a no free lunch situation for selection power when there is exact reconstructability in Appendix B, it seem that there is no theoretical guarantees on the power or explanations for that how the sliced-Wasserstein-based dependency regularization together with a novel perturbation technique introduced to reduce reconstructability can promote selection power.
It is not clear that how to enforce the swap property by the “multi-swapper” adversarial training procedure.

The motivation behind feature selection is high-dimensional data settings, which in my understanding means that the number of features is larger than the number of examples in the dataset. However, none of the simulated experiments include such scenario.
“Similar observations can be found in Figure 4.” seems to be “Similar observations can be found in Figure 5.” in the paragraph “Results” of section 4.4.

“Among them, model-specific ones such as AEknockoff (Liu & Zheng, 2018) Hidden Markov Model (HMM), knockoff (Sesia et al., 2017)” seems to be “Among them, model-specific ones such as AEknockoff (Liu & Zheng, 2018), Hidden Markov Model (HMM) knockoff (Sesia et al., 2017)” in the first paragraph of section 2.2.
More explanations for the proposed method striking a balance between FDR and power.
The diagram of DeepDRK pipeline and code library are given, but the algorithm for training objective (4) is not provided.
The paper faces criticism for its lack of clarity in presentation.

The paper faces criticism for the need for more rigorous experimental validation, particularly in terms of computational efficiency and the robustness of the results against different model configurations.
The paper lacks clarity in certain sections, particularly in the explanation of the methodology and the justification for specific design choices.
The computational efficiency of the proposed method is not discussed, which is crucial for practical applications.
The experimental validation is limited, with only a few datasets used to evaluate the method's effectiveness.
The paper does not adequately address the scalability of the method to large datasets or high-dimensional feature spaces.
There is a lack of discussion on the sensitivity of the method to different model configurations and hyperparameters, which could affect its robustness and generalizability.
The paper could benefit from a more detailed comparison with existing methods, especially in terms of the swap property and the performance metrics used.
Could the authors provide a more detailed explanation of the methodology, particularly the role and effectiveness of the multi-swapper adversarial training procedure?
How does the proposed method perform in terms of computational efficiency, and what are the practical implications of using this method in real-world applications?
Can the authors discuss the scalability of the method to large datasets or high-dimensional feature spaces, and provide insights into how the method could be adapted to handle such scenarios?
How sensitive is the method to different model configurations and hyperparameters, and what are the implications of these sensitivities for the robustness and generalizability of the method?
Could the authors provide a more detailed comparison with existing methods, especially in terms of the swap property and the performance metrics used?

In light of the concerns regarding the experimental validation, could the authors consider including more datasets or different experimental setups to validate the effectiveness of the method more robustly?
although there are concerns about the clarity of presentation and the need for more rigorous experimental validation.
The main concerns remaining are on the scalability of this method to real-world circuits as well as on the reliability of the AIG embeddings for retrieval at test time.

This work introduces, ABC-RL, an MCTS+Learning algorithm to optimize the transformation recipes in logic synthesis to minimize the area delay product (ADP). It employs GCN for AIG features and a transformer for applied transformations. It introduces a hyperparameter to adjust recommendations from pre-trained agents during the search. The hyperparameter is determined by similarity computed from GNN features learned during training. The key observation it leverages from hardware designs is that they contain both familiar and entirely new components. The empirical results show ABC-RL improves synthesized circuit Quality-of-Result (QoR) by up to 24.8% over SOTA methods and provides an average runtime speed-up of 1.6× compared to baseline MCTS.

It would be beneficial to provide an estimate of the number of gates in each benchmark circuit. One concern with this approach is whether GCN-based AIG embeddings can effectively scale to real-world circuit designs.
With the integration of GNN + transformer features. It is likely that the compute complexity and runtime of each search iteration would be higher than the baseline MCTS.
How is it compared to non-learning-based recipes? Is there an O3 flag for logic synthesis?
How does its wall clock time (not iterations) compare to other algorithms such as MCTS and SA+Pred?

The quality of AIG embeddings is the key to the entire methodology, but the authors only use a 3-layer GCN without exploring this issue. It's hard to believe such an approach could work properly, as circuit graphs are more complicated than plain graphs and have unique functions associated with them. Two AIGs could be structurally similar but functionally different, potentially requiring different synthesis recipes.

The experiments are conducted with a very small dataset (23 netlists for training), which is not convincing. The proposed agent should have seen sufficient circuit designs to develop a good RL strategy.
Please refer to the weakness part.

This papers proposes ABC-RL method to smoothly change the impact of learned policy in the search objective based on the graph similarity. Similar designs to training examples will have more bias from learned policy, while novel circuit will use more search. Compared to prior method, the propose one outperforms in various designs in terms of area-delay product.

The overall framework basically does test-time augmentation. It assumes pre-trained policy is not generalizable to novel circuits, and by comparing the test example with the training example, it dynamically selects among two search strategies, but in a smooth way. The circuit similarity is a performance proxy for pretrained agent and MCTS method, and use that proxy to predict the weights to ensemble two models. The novelty, in this sense, is limited. Is it possible to combine more synthesis strategies based on a more general performance predictor at test time?

The usage of BERT is not well justified. There are other simpler methods to encode variable-length sequences, e.g., RNN. The attention model is also data-hungry during training. Why BERT is the most suitable encoder?

The assumption that learned policy cannot generalize to diverse benchmarks is not well supported. If there is generalizable knowledge in circuit representation and synthesis strategies, it should try to improve the generalization of the learned policy by using more data/better algorithms. If this problem in nature is not generalizable or learnable, then it is not necessary to use an RL agent to learn the synthesis strategy at the beginning. The proposed method does not fundamentally explain or solve the learnability of circuit synthesis problems, but rather uses two model ensembles to just cover some in-distribution data with learned model and out-of-distribution examples by search.
Basically, it is listed in the weaknesses.

This paper proposes a new logical synthesis optimization sequence method ABC- RL based on MCTS and pertained policy. Unlike previous works, ABC-RL can compute the similarity of the new circuit with previous circuits, thus determining how much experience will be used. Experiments show that ABC-RL achieves SOTA optimization results in most circuits.
Some other ML method baselines are not included. For example, the DRiLLS [1] results are not included in the paper, but it is an important method recently.
The retrieval performance is not well reported. For example, it should give us examples of each circuit’s nearest neighbor circuit and similarity factor.
ChiPFormer [2] is an RL placement method with a pretrained policy and should be included in related work.
Could you compare ABC-RL and DRiLLS methods?
As weakness 2, could you give each circuit’s nest neighbor circuit and similarity factor?

The authors proposed a new method for finding optimal synthesis recipes for unseen netlists. They proposed three methods, one based on MCTS and a trained policy on training netlist (MCTS+L) and the other, and more superior, is using MCTS and a trained policy with a variable to control the reliance on the trained policy or the online MCTS search (ABC-RL). The variable α is determined based on a nearest neighbor search to determine if the netlist is close to the training netlists or not. The authors split the dataset of netlists into train, validation, and test sets and used the train to train the policy and used the validation set to set the hyperparameter that controls α. The experimental results show that the ABC-RL can outperform the other methods and SOTA in most of the test netlists and can achieve the best goe- mean performance.
The idea of using α was interesting and novel to the best of my knowledge, however it is a simple and small contribution.
It would be great if the authors can also provide the results for the rest of the benchmark netlists to ensure that the similar performance gains hold up for the training and validation sets.
In Section 2.4, in definition of σ_{δ_{th}, T}(z), replace θ with δ_{th}.
the paper has been critiqued for its limited novelty, lack of comprehensive comparisons with state-of-the-art methods, and insufficient experimental validation.
The paper lacks a detailed discussion on the limitations and potential negative societal impacts of the proposed method.
There is a significant gap in the experimental results compared to existing methods, particularly in terms of the QoR metric, which is not adequately explained.
The novelty of the approach is questionable as it seems to be a combination of existing methods without significant innovation.
The paper does not provide a detailed comparison with other state-of-the-art methods, which could have strengthened the paper's claims.
The methodology's reliance on similarity scores and the selection of benchmarks for training and testing raises concerns about the generalizability and robustness of the results.
The paper does not sufficiently address the scalability of the method to larger or more complex circuits, which is crucial for practical applications.
Can the authors provide more details on the limitations and potential negative societal impacts of their proposed method?
How does the proposed method compare in terms of runtime efficiency and QoR with other state-of-the-art methods, especially those that use different search algorithms or learning techniques?
Could the authors explain the discrepancies in the QoR results between their method and existing methods, and provide more insights into the selection of benchmarks for training and testing?
How does the method perform on larger or more complex circuits, and what are the potential challenges or limitations in scaling up the method?
